title: khachapuri
full_text: Main Eat Drink Reviews Insider At Home About Us Download Our Magazine Q2 2026 Q1 2026 Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026 Main Eat Drink Reviews Insider At Home About Us Download Our Magazine Q2 2026 Q1 2026 Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026 Now Reading: Khachapuri Is Just Bread and Cheese—Until It Isn’t 1 01 Khachapuri Is Just Bread and Cheese—Until It Isn’t Main// Eat// Drink// Reviews// Insider// At Home// About Us// Download Our Magazine// Q2 2026// Q1 2026// Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026// Home Eat Khachapuri Is Just Bread and Cheese—Until It Isn’tKhachapuri Is Just Bread and Cheese—Until It Isn’tColin GomezEat1 month agoShare It might seem like just bread and cheese on the surface, but khachapuri has a way of turning that simple idea into something that feels far more involved than it has any right to be. You know how some foods arrive on your table arranged with an almost sculptural precision? Mingling textures here, colours there, perhaps sprinkled with some unpronounceable herb or another. Yeah, well, the khachapuri is not one of them. It’s a big, artless, almost crass-looking thing, and that’s before you even learn what the ingredients are. If you’ve never seen one before, here’s an idea of what you’re dealing with: a boat-shaped bread filled with molten cheese. You take that and finish it with an egg yolk sitting right in the middle, alongside a generous knob of butter slowly melting into everything. Yes, it is every bit as indulgent as it sounds. No, there is no toned-down version of this that still makes sense. This, simply put, is the version. You need a steady pair of hands to make a good khachapuriPhotography: @rigi_gastrodouqan | Instagram Khachapuri comes from Georgia, and while there are a few variations depending on where you are, the one most people think of is the Adjaruli—the so-called “cheese boat”. You need only take a glance at it before understanding why this is the one that gets all the attention. But before you get too carried away admiring it, you should know that there’s a bit of a method to eating this thing. We can’t just have you diving in blindly, fork in hand, gouging chunks out like some sort of cheese-crazed berserker. There’s a process that needs to be adhered to, and once you get the hang of it, you’ll find it even becomes part of the appeal. A simple dish made with time-honoured techniquesPhotography: Yasin Onuş | Pexels First things first, you take that egg yolk and mix it into the cheese while everything is still piping hot. Then, in goes the butter, melting into the already molten centre and making the whole thing even richer, as if that was necessary. What you then do is you tear off pieces of the crust and use that to scoop everything up. That’ll be your utensil for the day. Leave all your cutlery and propriety at the door, because you absolutely won’t be needing it. It’s a dish that, for all its apparent unidimensionality, comes together quite well. The cheese is stretchy and savoury, with just enough of a tang to it to keep things from getting too heavy, while the bread manages to hold its shape just long enough before collapsing under the weight of its own indulgence. The edges offer a bit of bite, a nice contrast to its soft, decadent centre. It is, as first impressions would suggest, a decidedly rich affair, but not in a way that it ends up feeling like a chore to get through. There’s an underlying balance here, although it seems like it shouldn’t exist on paper. Megruli is another popular variant of khachapuriPhotography: khinkali.pub | Instagram But let’s not confine khachapuri to just Adjaruli, for that would be doing the cuisine a disservice. Other delectable, if not as dramatic, variants include Imeruli, a more enclosed form that bears a closer resemblance to a stuffed flatbread with the cheese sealed inside, as well as Megruli, which, in a move that I can only respect, adds cheese both inside and on top, because apparently one layer just wasn’t going to cut it. But regardless of the variation, the core idea remains the same: bread and cheese. And you know what? It doesn’t really need to be anything more than that. The great thing about khachapuri is that it knows exactly what it’s trying to do, and sets about doing it without the slightest shred of hesitation. It’s a dish built for enjoyment, and one that doesn’t have time in its day for decorum, subtlety, or any of that sort of faff. And, like all dishes of its ilk, it’s best enjoyed shared, ideally eaten somewhere in that very thin margin where it’s still properly hot, but just enough that eating it won’t erase your fingertips in the process. Traditional Georgian cheese renders the dish indulgent yet balancedPhotography: fooddrinkdestinations.com Now, Georgian food isn’t exactly something you’ll stumble across on every corner, especially not in our neck of the woods. But having said that, you can still find it if you look hard enough. Astana Restaurant in TTDI is one such establishment you could go to. So, if you ever find yourself in the mood for something big and unapologetically cheesy, as I so often do, do yourself a favour and order a plate. Bring your crew along while you’re at it. It’s meant to be shared, and you’ll be able to avoid having to look at yourself in the mirror and answering some difficult questions afterwards. At the end of the day, khachapuri is just bread and cheese. But it is also, very clearly, not just bread and cheese. And once you’ve had it done right, you’ll understand exactly what I mean by that.
